articles.title: How China May Have Sought to Recruit a House Aide to Spy on the U.S.
articles.summary: A staff member on the House China Committee was promised $10,000 for U.S. policy insights, on issues like Venezuela and rare-earth minerals.

articles.title: How China’s Leader Lost Faith in His Generals
articles.summary: Xi Jinping spent 13 years building a military to rival that of the United States. But the stronger the Chinese forces grew, the less he trusted the generals he had handpicked to run them.

articles.title: Organizers of a canceled human rights conference in Zambia say China intervened
articles.summary: Zambia was due to host Rights Con, the world's biggest digital rights gathering, this week -- but abruptly canceled the event. Organizers blame Chinese pressure for the decision.

articles.title: Frontier Airlines plane strikes and kills pedestrian
articles.summary: Denver International Airport said the person had jumped a fence and dashed into the aircraft's path minutes before being struck.

articles.title: Republicans Are Building an Advantage in Redistricting. How Much Is It Worth?
articles.summary: Where things stands in the race for House control after recent court rulings.

articles.title: An expert on Iranian politics reviews the status of negotiations to end the war on Iran
articles.summary: NPR's Elissa Nadworny talks to Mehrzad Boroujerdi of the Missouri University of Science and Technology about the status of the Trump Administration's negotiations to end the war on Iran.

articles.title: Revisiting Guy Goma's iconic accidental BBC interview 20 years ago this week
articles.summary: NPR's Elissa Nadworny talks to Guy Goma who thought he was being interviewed for an IT job at the BBC but instead found himself live on air. His interview 20 years ago became a viral sensation.

articles.title: Why saying hello to strangers can be good for you
articles.summary: A study establishes that "social ties" — a fancy way of saying being nice to other, even those you don't know — has benefits. A teacher asked her students to test the thesis in real life.

articles.title: They graduate to six figure salaries, and grueling work
articles.summary: Cadets from the nation's Merchant Marine academies are finding lots of demand and great salaries because of a shortage of licensed mariners.

articles.title: Southern Republicans redistrict after Supreme Court rules, Dems lose big in Virginia
articles.summary: The Supreme Court weakened minority voting rights and prompted Republicans in four states to move to redistrict as part of Trump's push. A court nullified Democratic redistricting in Virginia.

articles.title: Soccer was once considered niche in the U.S. Then came the 1994 World Cup
articles.summary: Soccer — or football as it's known around the globe — was far from mainstream in the U.S. leading up to the 1994 World Cup. But in the end, the tournament was considered a resounding success. How exac

articles.title: Moscow marks Victory Day with a Red Square parade under tight security
articles.summary: Security was tight in Moscow as Putin and several foreign leaders attended the parade, even as a U.S.-brokered three-day ceasefire eased concerns about possible Ukrainian attempts to disrupt the festi

articles.title: How Republicans Gained an Edge on the Midterm House Map Over 10 Days
articles.summary: Republicans are charging ahead in the nation’s redistricting race, and showing new bullishness after months of growing midterm fears.

articles.title: How Minority Districts Fueled the G.O.P.’s Southern Ascendancy in Congress
articles.summary: The “majority-minority” House districts struck down by the Supreme Court last week sent a surge of Black and Hispanic lawmakers to Congress. They also opened opportunities for the G.O.P.

articles.title: Two Court Decisions Have Unleashed an Era of Perpetual Redistricting
articles.summary: Four states are considering drawing new maps in the coming weeks. Another dozen or more could join the fray next year.

articles.title: Long Overlooked, Caspian Sea Provides Strategic Trade Route for Iran
articles.summary: The landlocked body of water has taken on new significance, with Russia shipping military and commercial goods to bolster Tehran’s ability to withstand the U.S. assault.

articles.title: Taiwan’s Plastic Habit Collides With Shortages Caused by a Faraway War
articles.summary: Taiwan, one of the world’s biggest plastic users, is facing supply disruptions caused by the war in Iran. The crunch is already trickling down into everyday life.

articles.title: What is a radical? It's the question of M.I.A.'s vexing career
articles.summary: Fans who danced to "Paper Planes" might hardly recognize the conspiracy-touting artist before them today — but in a certain way, she's the same button-pusher as ever.

articles.title: Trump says Russia and Ukraine have agreed to his request for a 3-day ceasefire
articles.summary: President Volodymyr Zelenskyy and Yuri Ushakov, President Vladimir Putin's foreign affairs adviser, both confirmed the agreement for a three-day ceasefire and an exchange of prisoners.

articles.title: Russia's Victory Day celebrations are dampened by ongoing war with Ukraine
articles.summary: World War 2 Victory Day celebrations in Moscow will be muted this year because of fears of drone attacks, as the public tires of the Ukraine war.

articles.title: Colombia's rogue hippos could find refuge in India
articles.summary: In Colombia, a plan to cull Pablo Escobar's invasive hippos is challenged by an Indian billionaire's offer to relocate dozens of the animals to India's wildlife reserve instead.

articles.title: U.S. intercepts Iranian attacks on 3 ships. And, what to know about hantavirus
articles.summary: The U.S. says it intercepted Iranian attacks yesterday targeting three Navy ships. And, what to know about the hantavirus outbreak that started on a cruise ship.

articles.title: Dominion still has pending lawsuits against election deniers such as Rudy Giuliani and Sidney Powell
